model_narrative: We are choosing which items to pack into a single knapsack. For each item we decide whether or not to include it. Every item carries a known profit if we take it and a known weight that it adds to the load. We also track the total profit collected from the items we choose. The objective is to maximize the total profit of the selected items.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the set of candidate items available to place into the knapsack, indexed by label

params.name: p
params.doc: the profit gained from including each item in the selection, in value units per item

params.name: w
params.doc: the weight each item contributes if it is included, in weight units per item

params.name: c
params.doc: the total weight capacity of the knapsack, in weight units; a single scalar

vars.name: x
vars.doc: selection indicator for each item; equals 1 if the item is placed in the knapsack and 0 otherwise

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total profit accumulated from the selected items, in value units; a single scalar

constraints.name: w[i]
constraints.intent: The items packed into the knapsack cannot weigh more than it can carry. Adding up the weight contributed by every item that is selected, the total must not exceed the knapsack's weight capacity.

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total profit being tracked has to reflect exactly the items that were chosen. Summing the profit of every selected item, set the tracked total profit equal to that sum.

query: Items i8 and i9 ship in the same oversized crate, and the warehouse can only stage one of those crates at a time this cycle. There's a proposal on the table to bake that into the plan, so at most one of i8 or i9 ends up in the load. Plug that in and tell me what total profit the packing comes back with.

query: The two premium pieces, i9 and i10, each need a protective spacer, and we only carry spacers salvaged from the low-grade filler items i5 and i7. So the count of premium items packed can't exceed the count of filler items packed from that pair. We're looking at running the plan under that pairing — what does the optimizer return for total profit?

query: Merchandising is pitching two bundling rules for this run, and they want them applied together: i10 may only ride along if its anchor i1 is also packed, and likewise i8 may only go in if its anchor i7 is packed. Apply both bundling rules at once and walk me through the total profit the model lands on.
expected_pyomo:
model.i10_needs_i1 = Constraint(expr=model.x['i10'] <= model.x['i1'])
model.i8_needs_i7 = Constraint(expr=model.x['i8'] <= model.x['i7'])

query: Handling cost goes up with the number of distinct items we pack, so we're looking at holding the load to no more than 4 items total. With that cap in place, what total profit does the model return?
expected_pyomo: model.item_count_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i] for i in model.i) <= 4)

query: Compliance has a rule that premium item i9 can't be shipped on its own — it always has to travel alongside its companion piece i6. Right now the plan grabs i9 but leaves i6 sitting in the warehouse, which would never clear inspection. Shouldn't i9 only be allowed in the load when i6 is in there too? What's the model seeing that lets it ignore that pairing?

query: Sales keeps griping that the same overlooked four — i1, i5, i6 and i7 — almost never make it into a load, and they want a stronger presence for that group. Wouldn't it be reasonable to insist that at least three of those four overlooked items show up in any packing? What's the tradeoff the optimizer is making by leaving nearly all of them out?

query: The plan loads up on the flashy premium pieces i9 and i10 while the bulky workhorses i1, i6 and i7 get shut out completely. Our floor team argues the bulky group should never be outnumbered by the premium pair — they'd want at least as many bulky items packed as premium ones. Couldn't we hold the line that way? What's pulling the optimizer so hard toward the premium side?
expected_pyomo: model.bulky_not_outnumbered = Constraint(expr=model.x['i1'] + model.x['i6'] + model.x['i7'] >= model.x['i9'] + model.x['i10'])

query: We've got three big-ticket items — i1, i6 and i7 — that customers keep asking about, yet the plan walks past all three. Surely we can find space for a couple of them; shouldn't any packing carry at least two of those three headline items? What's keeping the model from making that move?
